skill.name: standards-extraction
skill.description: Extract project coding standards from contribution guides, editor settings, formatter configs, linter configs, hooks, and CI references.
skill.summary: Extract project coding standards and contribution conventions from repository documentation and configuration files.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, JSON examples, and a schema URL as command, weak crypto, and network risks. Review found these are documentation-only false positives with no executable scripts, no data exfiltration, and no prompt injection evidence; one broken cookbook reference remains a low-risk quality issue.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged backticks wrap filenames, directory names, example configuration blocks, and integration labels. They do not execute shell commands or invoke a runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown tables or fenced examples, not source files that execute commands. No shell script, Ruby code, or dynamic command construction is present in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is File Extension Text
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The high-severity weak crypto alerts correspond to ordinary documentation text such as CONTRIBUTING.md and example branch naming, not MD5 or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines do not contain hashing APIs, crypto libraries, or security-sensitive validation. The match appears to come from short text patterns inside documentation.

low_findings.title: Schema URL Is Not Network Activity
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URL appears inside an output schema example and does not instruct the agent to fetch or transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a fenced JSON schema example. There is no network client, request instruction, credential handling, or external endpoint for data transfer.

low_findings.title: Missing Cookbook File Reference
low_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to read and execute ./cookbook/config-parsing.md, but the scanned file structure only includes cookbook/README.md. This can cause workflow failure or unsupported behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced path is visible in the skill instructions, and the provided file structure does not list that file. This is a reliability issue, not evidence of malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when project conventions are scattered across guides and configuration files. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code summarize those rules into practical contribution guidance.
actual_capabilities: Checks common contribution guide locations such as CONTRIBUTING.md and .github/CONTRIBUTING.md.
Extracts commit format, branch naming, pull request process, code style notes, and testing requirements.
Reads formatting conventions from .editorconfig, Prettier, Ruff, Black, and similar configuration files.
Summarizes linter tools, extended configs, enabled rules, disabled rules, and custom rule notes.
Reports git hook, pre-commit, Husky, pull request template, and CI check references when present.
limitations: It depends on repository files being present and readable in the current workspace.
It may miss conventions that are only enforced in external services or private documentation.
It does not validate whether every extracted rule is actively enforced by tooling.
The referenced cookbook/config-parsing.md helper is not included in the scanned package.

use_cases.title: Onboard a New Contributor
use_cases.description: Create a concise guide to commit style, formatting, linting, tests, and pull request expectations before a first change.

use_cases.title: Prepare Automation Defaults
use_cases.description: Extract formatter and linter settings so coding agents can generate changes that match the repository conventions.

use_cases.title: Audit Documentation Drift
use_cases.description: Compare contribution guidance with configuration files to identify missing, conflicting, or outdated project rules.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the standards-extraction skill on this repository. Summarize contribution rules, formatting settings, linting tools, hooks, and required checks in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Create Agent Instructions
prompt_templates.scenario: Configure Claude, Codex, or Claude Code for a project
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the project standards and turn them into concise instructions for an AI coding agent. Include exact sources for each rule when available.

prompt_templates.title: Find Conflicting Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze contribution docs and configuration files for conflicting or missing standards. Separate confirmed rules from assumptions and list the source files.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Compliance Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract repository standards and create a pull request compliance checklist. Group items by formatting, linting, tests, commit style, branches, hooks, and CI checks.

output_examples.input: A repository with CONTRIBUTING.md, .editorconfig, .prettierrc, and ESLint configuration.
output: Contribution rules: conventional commits, feature branches, review required before merge.
Formatting rules: spaces, two-space indentation, final newline, single quotes, semicolons enabled.
Quality checks: ESLint and Prettier should run before pull request submission.

output_examples.input: A Python repository with pyproject.toml, Ruff settings, Black settings, and pre-commit hooks.
output: Python style: Ruff selects import and error checks, Black controls line length.
Hooks: pre-commit runs formatting and lint checks before commits.
Contributor note: run the same checks locally before opening a pull request.

output_examples.input: A mixed repository where contribution docs and formatter settings disagree.
output: Conflict found: documentation says one indentation rule, while editor settings define another.
Recommended action: treat configuration as the enforced rule and update documentation.
Evidence should include the exact files that contain each rule.

best_practices: Run the skill from the repository root so standard paths resolve correctly.
Ask for source file references when using the output for contributor documentation.
Review conflicts manually before changing formatter, linter, or pull request policy files.
anti_patterns: Do not infer project conventions from language defaults when configuration files are available.
Do not treat example output as the repository standard without checking local files.
Do not use the result as a security or compliance certification.

faq.question: What files does this skill inspect?
faq.answer: It focuses on contribution guides, EditorConfig, formatter configs, linter configs, git hooks, pull request templates, and CI references.

faq.question: Can it create a contributor guide?
faq.answer: Yes. It can summarize extracted standards into onboarding guidance when the relevant source files exist.

faq.question: Does it run linters or formatters?
faq.answer: No. The skill describes extraction steps and reports configuration. It does not execute project tooling by itself.

faq.question: Can it detect conflicting standards?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare documentation and configuration files and report conflicts with source references.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: The reviewed skill contains no confirmed network transfer logic. Users should still review generated summaries before sharing them externally.

faq.question: What is the main package quality issue?
faq.answer: The skill references cookbook/config-parsing.md, but that file was not present in the scanned package.
